A light bulb is rated at 100W for a 220 V supply. Find

  1. the resistance of the bulb;
  2. the peak voltage of the source; and
  3. the rms current through the bulb.
question_text: A light bulb is rated at 100W for a 220 V supply. Find the resistance of the bulb; the peak voltage of the source; and the rms current through the bulb.
explanation: We are given P = 100 W and V = 220 V. The resistance of the bulb is {tex}R=\frac{V^{2}}{P}=\frac{(220 \mathrm{V})^{2}}{100 \mathrm{W}}=484 \Omega{/tex} The peak voltage of the source is {tex}v_{m}=\sqrt{2} V=311 \mathrm{V} {/tex} Since, P = lV thus rms cureent is given by, {tex}I=\frac{P}{V}=\frac{100 \mathrm{W}}{220 \mathrm{V}}{/tex} = 0.454 A
solution: We are given P = 100 W and V = 220 V. The resistance of the bulb is {tex}R=\frac{V^{2}}{P}=\frac{(220 \mathrm{V})^{2}}{100 \mathrm{W}}=484 \Omega{/tex} The peak voltage of the source is {tex}v_{m}=\sqrt{2} V=311 \mathrm{V} {/tex} Since, P = lV thus rms cureent is given by, {tex}I=\frac{P}{V}=\frac{100 \mathrm{W}}{220 \mathrm{V}}{/tex} = 0.454 A
solution_html:
1. We are given P = 100 W and V = 220 V. The resistance of the bulb is 
    {tex}R=\frac{V^{2}}{P}=\frac{(220 \mathrm{V})^{2}}{100 \mathrm{W}}=484 \Omega{/tex}
  2. The peak voltage of the source is
    {tex}v_{m}=\sqrt{2} V=311 \mathrm{V} {/tex}
  3. Since, P = lV   thus rms cureent is given by,
    {tex}I=\frac{P}{V}=\frac{100 \mathrm{W}}{220 \mathrm{V}}{/tex} = 0.454 A

  3. Why the primary and secondary coils of a transformer are preferably wound on the same core?
question_text: What is the principle of the transformer? Explain how laminating the core a transformer helps to reduce eddy current losses in it? Why the primary and secondary coils of a transformer are preferably wound on the same core?
explanation: A transformer works on the principle of mutual induction between the two circuits which is linked by a common magnetic flux. (Alternatively-an emf is induced in the secondary coil when the magnetic flux, linked with it changes with time due to a (time) changing magnetic flux linked with the primary coil). Laminations are thin, making the resistance higher. Eddy currents are confined within each thin lamination. This reduces the net eddy current. For maximum sharing of magnetic flux and magnetic flux per turn to be the same in both primary and secondary.
solution: A transformer works on the principle of mutual induction between the two circuits which is linked by a common magnetic flux. (Alternatively-an emf is induced in the secondary coil when the magnetic flux, linked with it changes with time due to a (time) changing magnetic flux linked with the primary coil). Laminations are thin, making the resistance higher. Eddy currents are confined within each thin lamination. This reduces the net eddy current. For maximum sharing of magnetic flux and magnetic flux per turn to be the same in both primary and secondary.
solution_html:
1. A transformer works on the principle of mutual induction between the two circuits which is linked by a common magnetic flux. (Alternatively-an emf is induced in the secondary coil when the magnetic flux, linked with it changes with time due to a (time) changing magnetic flux linked with the primary coil).
  2. Laminations are thin, making the resistance higher. Eddy currents are confined within each thin lamination. This reduces the net eddy current.
  3. For maximum sharing of magnetic flux and magnetic flux per turn to be the same in both primary and secondary.

subject: Physics
question_html: When an ideal capacitor is connected to an a.c. source, show that the average power supplied by the source over a complete cycle is Zero.
question_text: When an ideal capacitor is connected to an a.c. source, show that the average power supplied by the source over a complete cycle is Zero.
explanation: Average power associated with a capacitor: When an a.c. is applied to a capacitor, the current leads the voltage in phase by {tex}\pi \over 2{/tex} radian. So we write the expressions for instantaneous voltage and current as follows: {tex}V=V_{0} \sin \omega t{/tex} and {tex}I=I_{0} \sin \left(\omega t+\frac{\pi}{2}\right)=I_{0} \cos \omega t{/tex} Work done in the circuit in small time dt will be dW = Pdt = VIdt = V 0 I 0 sin {tex}\omega{/tex} t cos {tex}\omega{/tex} t dt {tex}=\frac{V_{0} I_{0}}{2} \sin 2 \omega t d t{/tex} The average power dissipated per cycle in the capacitor is Pav = {tex}\frac{W}{T}=\frac{1}{T} \int_{0}^{T} d W=\frac{V_{0} I_{0}}{2 T} \int_{0}^{T} \sin 2 \omega t d t{/tex} {tex}=\frac{V_{0} I_{0}}{2 T}\left[-\frac{\cos 2 \omega t}{2 \omega}\right]_{0}^{T}{/tex} {tex}=-\frac{V_{0} I_{0}}{4 T \omega}\left[\cos \frac{4 \pi}{T} t\right]_{0}^{T}{/tex} {tex}=-\frac{V_{0} I_{0}}{4 T \omega}[\cos 4 \pi-\cos 0]{/tex} {tex}=-\frac{V_{0} I_{0}}{4 T \omega}[1-1]=0{/tex} Thus the average power dissipated per cycle in a capacitor is zero.
solution: Average power associated with a capacitor: When an a.c. is applied to a capacitor, the current leads the voltage in phase by {tex}\pi \over 2{/tex} radian. So we write the expressions for instantaneous voltage and current as follows: {tex}V=V_{0} \sin \omega t{/tex} and {tex}I=I_{0} \sin \left(\omega t+\frac{\pi}{2}\right)=I_{0} \cos \omega t{/tex} Work done in the circuit in small time dt will be dW = Pdt = VIdt = V 0 I 0 sin {tex}\omega{/tex} t cos {tex}\omega{/tex} t dt {tex}=\frac{V_{0} I_{0}}{2} \sin 2 \omega t d t{/tex} The average power dissipated per cycle in the capacitor is Pav = {tex}\frac{W}{T}=\frac{1}{T} \int_{0}^{T} d W=\frac{V_{0} I_{0}}{2 T} \int_{0}^{T} \sin 2 \omega t d t{/tex} {tex}=\frac{V_{0} I_{0}}{2 T}\left[-\frac{\cos 2 \omega t}{2 \omega}\right]_{0}^{T}{/tex} {tex}=-\frac{V_{0} I_{0}}{4 T \omega}\left[\cos \frac{4 \pi}{T} t\right]_{0}^{T}{/tex} {tex}=-\frac{V_{0} I_{0}}{4 T \omega}[\cos 4 \pi-\cos 0]{/tex} {tex}=-\frac{V_{0} I_{0}}{4 T \omega}[1-1]=0{/tex} Thus the average power dissipated per cycle in a capacitor is zero.
solution_html:
Average power associated with a capacitor: When an a.c. is applied to a capacitor, the current leads the voltage in phase by {tex}\pi \over 2{/tex} radian. So we write the expressions for instantaneous voltage and current as follows:
{tex}V=V_{0} \sin \omega t{/tex}
and {tex}I=I_{0} \sin \left(\omega t+\frac{\pi}{2}\right)=I_{0} \cos \omega t{/tex}
Work done in the circuit in small time dt will be
dW = Pdt = VIdt = V0I0 sin {tex}\omega{/tex}t cos {tex}\omega{/tex}t dt
{tex}=\frac{V_{0} I_{0}}{2} \sin 2 \omega t d t{/tex}
The average power dissipated per cycle in the capacitor is
Pav = {tex}\frac{W}{T}=\frac{1}{T} \int_{0}^{T} d W=\frac{V_{0} I_{0}}{2 T} \int_{0}^{T} \sin 2 \omega t d t{/tex} 
{tex}=\frac{V_{0} I_{0}}{2 T}\left[-\frac{\cos 2 \omega t}{2 \omega}\right]_{0}^{T}{/tex}
{tex}=-\frac{V_{0} I_{0}}{4 T \omega}\left[\cos \frac{4 \pi}{T} t\right]_{0}^{T}{/tex}
{tex}=-\frac{V_{0} I_{0}}{4 T \omega}[\cos 4 \pi-\cos 0]{/tex}
{tex}=-\frac{V_{0} I_{0}}{4 T \omega}[1-1]=0{/tex} 
Thus the average power dissipated per cycle in a capacitor is zero.

A step-up down transformer operated on a 2.5 kV line. It supplies a load with 20 A. The ratio of the primary winding to the secondary is 10 : 1. If the transformer is 90% efficient, calculate

  1. the power output
  2. the voltage and

question_text: A step-up down transformer operated on a 2.5 kV line. It supplies a load with 20 A. The ratio of the primary winding to the secondary is 10 : 1. If the transformer is 90% efficient, calculate the power output the voltage and the current in the secondary coil.
explanation: Given: {tex}V _ { P } = 2.5 \times 10 ^ { 3 } \mathrm { V }{/tex} , Input current, {tex}I _ { P } = 20 \mathrm { A }{/tex} Also, {tex}\frac{N _ { p }} {N _ { s}}= \frac{10 }{1}{/tex} Output Power = {tex}\frac{{90}}{{100}} \times \left( {2.5 \times {{10}^3}} \right) \times (20{\text{)}} = 4.5 \times {10^4}{\text{W}}{/tex} {tex}\frac {V_s}{V_p} = \frac {N_s}{N_p} \Rightarrow {V_s} = \frac {N_s}{N_p} \times {V_p}{/tex} Voltage, {tex}V _ { S } = \frac { 1 } { 10 } \times 2.5 \times 10 ^ { 3 } \mathrm { } = 250 \mathrm { V }{/tex} {tex}V _ { \mathrm { s } } I _ { \mathrm { s } } = 4.5 \times 10 ^ { 4 } \mathrm { W }{/tex} Current, {tex}I _ { S } = \frac { 4.5 \times 10 ^ { 4 } } { V _ { S } } = \frac { 4.5 \times 10 ^ { 4 } } { 250 }{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad I _ { S } = 180 \mathrm { A }{/tex}
solution: Given: {tex}V _ { P } = 2.5 \times 10 ^ { 3 } \mathrm { V }{/tex} , Input current, {tex}I _ { P } = 20 \mathrm { A }{/tex} Also, {tex}\frac{N _ { p }} {N _ { s}}= \frac{10 }{1}{/tex} Output Power = {tex}\frac{{90}}{{100}} \times \left( {2.5 \times {{10}^3}} \right) \times (20{\text{)}} = 4.5 \times {10^4}{\text{W}}{/tex} {tex}\frac {V_s}{V_p} = \frac {N_s}{N_p} \Rightarrow {V_s} = \frac {N_s}{N_p} \times {V_p}{/tex} Voltage, {tex}V _ { S } = \frac { 1 } { 10 } \times 2.5 \times 10 ^ { 3 } \mathrm { } = 250 \mathrm { V }{/tex} {tex}V _ { \mathrm { s } } I _ { \mathrm { s } } = 4.5 \times 10 ^ { 4 } \mathrm { W }{/tex} Current, {tex}I _ { S } = \frac { 4.5 \times 10 ^ { 4 } } { V _ { S } } = \frac { 4.5 \times 10 ^ { 4 } } { 250 }{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad I _ { S } = 180 \mathrm { A }{/tex}
solution_html:
1. Given: {tex}V _ { P } = 2.5 \times 10 ^ { 3 } \mathrm { V }{/tex}, Input current, {tex}I _ { P } = 20 \mathrm { A }{/tex}
    Also, {tex}\frac{N _ { p }} {N _ { s}}= \frac{10 }{1}{/tex}
    Output Power = {tex}\frac{{90}}{{100}} \times \left( {2.5 \times {{10}^3}} \right) \times (20{\text{)}} = 4.5 \times {10^4}{\text{W}}{/tex}
  2. {tex}\frac {V_s}{V_p} = \frac {N_s}{N_p} \Rightarrow {V_s} = \frac {N_s}{N_p} \times {V_p}{/tex}
    Voltage, {tex}V _ { S } = \frac { 1 } { 10 } \times 2.5 \times 10 ^ { 3 } \mathrm { } = 250 \mathrm { V }{/tex}
  3. {tex}V _ { \mathrm { s } } I _ { \mathrm { s } } = 4.5 \times 10 ^ { 4 } \mathrm { W }{/tex}
    Current, {tex}I _ { S } = \frac { 4.5 \times 10 ^ { 4 } } { V _ { S } } = \frac { 4.5 \times 10 ^ { 4 } } { 250 }{/tex}
    {tex}\Rightarrow \quad I _ { S } = 180 \mathrm { A }{/tex}

question_text: A device X is connected to an a.c source. The variation of voltage, current and power in one complete cycle is shown in Figure. Which curve shows power consumption over a full cycle? What is the average power consumption over a cycle? Identify the device X .
explanation: We know that Power is the product of voltage and current (Power = P = VI) So, the curve of power will be having the maximum amplitude, equals to the product of amplitudes of voltage (V) and current (I) curve. Frequencies, of B and C are-equal, therefore they represent V and I curves. So, the curve A represents power consumption over a full cycle. The full cycle of the graph (as shown by the shaded area in the diagram) consists of one positive and one negative symmetrical area. Hence, average power consumption over a cycle is zero. Here phase difference between V and I is {tex}\pi{/tex} / 2 therefore, the device 'X' may be an inductor (L) or capacitor (C) or the series combination of L and C.

question_text: When an AC source is connected to an ideal inductor show that the average power supplied by the source over a complete cycle is zero. A lamp is connected in series with an inductor and an AC source. What happens to the brightness of the lamp when the key is plugged in and an iron rod is inserted inside the inductor? Explain.
explanation: As {tex}P _ { { av } } = V _ { { rms } } I _ { { rms } } \cos \phi{/tex} In an ideal inductor, rms current I rms lags behind the applied rms voltage V rms by {tex}\frac{\pi}{2}{/tex} {tex}\therefore \quad \phi = \frac{\pi}{2}{/tex} {tex}P _ { a v } = V _ { rm s } I _ { r m s } \cos \frac{\pi}{2}{/tex} or {tex}P _ { { av } } = V _ { { rms } } I _ { { rms } } \times { 0 }{/tex} or {tex}P _ { a v } = 0{/tex} The brightness of the lamp decreases. It is because when iron rod is inserted inside the inductor, its inductance L increases(as inductance depends directly on the permeability of the material inside it i.e., {tex}L\propto\mu{/tex} , thereby increasing its inductive reactance X L as permeability of iron rod is greater than that of free space or air and hence impedance Z of the circuit. As I rms = V rms /Z, so, this decreases the current I rms in the circuit and hence decreases the brightness of lamp.

As I rms = V rms /Z, so, this decreases the current I rms in the circuit and hence decreases the brightness of lamp.
solution_html:
1. As {tex}P _ { { av } } = V _ { { rms } } I _ { { rms } } \cos \phi{/tex}
    In an ideal inductor, rms current  Irms lags behind the applied rms voltage Vrms by {tex}\frac{\pi}{2}{/tex}
    {tex}\therefore \quad \phi = \frac{\pi}{2}{/tex}
    {tex}P _ { a v } = V _ { rm s } I _ { r m s } \cos \frac{\pi}{2}{/tex}
    or {tex}P _ { { av } } = V _ { { rms } } I _ { { rms } } \times { 0 }{/tex}
    or {tex}P _ { a v } = 0{/tex}
  2. The brightness of the lamp decreases. It is because when iron rod is inserted inside the inductor, its inductance L increases(as inductance depends directly on the permeability of the material inside it i.e., {tex}L\propto\mu{/tex}, thereby increasing its inductive reactance XL as permeability of iron rod is greater than that of free space or air and hence impedance Z of the circuit. As Irms = Vrms/Z, so, this decreases the current Irms in the circuit and hence decreases the brightness of lamp.

question_html: Mention various energy losses in a transformer.
question_text: Mention various energy losses in a transformer.
explanation: The various losses in a transformer are: (i) Flux Leakage: There is always some flux leakage; that is, not all of the flux due to primary passes through the secondary due to poor design of the core or the air gaps in the core. It can be reduced by winding the primary and secondary coils one over the other. (ii) Resistance of the windings: The wire used for the winding has some resistance and so, energy is lost due to heat produced in the wire (I 2 R). In high current, low voltage windings, these are minimised by using thick wire. (iii) Eddy currents : The alternating magnetic flux induces eddy currents in the iron core and causes heating. The effect is reduced by using a laminated core. (iv) Hysteresis: The magnetisation of the core is repeatedly reversed by the alternating magnetic field. The resulting expenditure of energy in the core appears as heat and is kept to a minimum by using a magnetic material which has a low hysteresis loss.
solution: The various losses in a transformer are: (i) Flux Leakage: There is always some flux leakage; that is, not all of the flux due to primary passes through the secondary due to poor design of the core or the air gaps in the core. It can be reduced by winding the primary and secondary coils one over the other. (ii) Resistance of the windings: The wire used for the winding has some resistance and so, energy is lost due to heat produced in the wire (I 2 R). In high current, low voltage windings, these are minimised by using thick wire. (iii) Eddy currents : The alternating magnetic flux induces eddy currents in the iron core and causes heating. The effect is reduced by using a laminated core. (iv) Hysteresis: The magnetisation of the core is repeatedly reversed by the alternating magnetic field. The resulting expenditure of energy in the core appears as heat and is kept to a minimum by using a magnetic material which has a low hysteresis loss.
solution_html:
The various losses in a transformer are:
(i) Flux Leakage: There is always some flux leakage; that is, not all of the flux due to primary passes through the secondary due to poor design of the core or the air gaps in the core. It can be reduced by winding the primary and secondary coils one over the other.
(ii) Resistance of the windings: The wire used for the winding has some resistance and so, energy is lost due to heat produced in the wire (I2R). In high current, low voltage windings, these are minimised by using thick wire.
(iii) Eddy currents: The alternating magnetic flux induces eddy currents in the iron core and causes heating. The effect is reduced by using a laminated core.
(iv) Hysteresis: The magnetisation of the core is repeatedly reversed by the alternating magnetic field. The resulting expenditure of energy in the core appears as heat and is kept to a minimum by using a magnetic material which has a low hysteresis loss.

question_html: A circuit draws a power of 550 W from a source of 220 V, 50 Hz. The power factor of the circuit is 0.8. The current in the circuit lags behind the voltage. Show that a capacitor of about {tex}\frac{1}{42 \pi} \times 10^{-2}{/tex} will have to be connected to bring its power factor to unity.
question_text: A circuit draws a power of 550 W from a source of 220 V, 50 Hz. The power factor of the circuit is 0.8. The current in the circuit lags behind the voltage. Show that a capacitor of about {tex}\frac{1}{42 \pi} \times 10^{-2}{/tex} will have to be connected to bring its power factor to unity.
explanation: As {tex}P_{a v}=V_{e f f} \cdot I_{e f f} \cos \phi{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}I_{e f f}=\frac{P_{a v}}{V_{e f f} \cos \phi}=\frac{550}{220 \times 0.8}=\frac{25}{8} \mathrm{~A}{/tex} {tex}R=\frac{P_{a v}}{I_{f f}^{2}}=\frac{550 \times 8 \times 8}{25 \times 25}=\frac{22 \times 64}{25} \Omega{/tex} {tex}\left[\because P_{a v}=I_{e f f}^{2} R\right]{/tex} {tex}\tan \phi=\frac{\sin \phi}{\cos \phi}=\frac{\sqrt{1-(0.8)^{2}}}{0.8}=\frac{0.6}{0.8}=\frac{3}{4}{/tex} But {tex}\tan \phi=\frac{X_{L}}{R}{/tex} {tex}X_{L}=\tan \phi . R=\frac{3}{4} \times \frac{22 \times 64}{25} \simeq 42 \Omega{/tex} For power factor to be unity. {tex}X_{L}=X_{C}{/tex} or {tex}\omega L=\frac{1}{\omega C}{/tex} or {tex}C=\frac{1}{\omega^{2} L}=\frac{1}{\omega X_{L}}=\frac{1}{2 \pi f} \cdot \frac{1}{X_{L}}=\frac{1}{100 \pi} \cdot \frac{1}{42}{/tex} [ {tex}\because{/tex} {tex}\omega L=X_{L}{/tex} ] or {tex}C=\frac{1}{42 \pi} \times 10^{-2} \mathrm{~F}{/tex}
solution: As {tex}P_{a v}=V_{e f f} \cdot I_{e f f} \cos \phi{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}I_{e f f}=\frac{P_{a v}}{V_{e f f} \cos \phi}=\frac{550}{220 \times 0.8}=\frac{25}{8} \mathrm{~A}{/tex} {tex}R=\frac{P_{a v}}{I_{f f}^{2}}=\frac{550 \times 8 \times 8}{25 \times 25}=\frac{22 \times 64}{25} \Omega{/tex} {tex}\left[\because P_{a v}=I_{e f f}^{2} R\right]{/tex} {tex}\tan \phi=\frac{\sin \phi}{\cos \phi}=\frac{\sqrt{1-(0.8)^{2}}}{0.8}=\frac{0.6}{0.8}=\frac{3}{4}{/tex} But {tex}\tan \phi=\frac{X_{L}}{R}{/tex} {tex}X_{L}=\tan \phi . R=\frac{3}{4} \times \frac{22 \times 64}{25} \simeq 42 \Omega{/tex} For power factor to be unity. {tex}X_{L}=X_{C}{/tex} or {tex}\omega L=\frac{1}{\omega C}{/tex} or {tex}C=\frac{1}{\omega^{2} L}=\frac{1}{\omega X_{L}}=\frac{1}{2 \pi f} \cdot \frac{1}{X_{L}}=\frac{1}{100 \pi} \cdot \frac{1}{42}{/tex} [ {tex}\because{/tex} {tex}\omega L=X_{L}{/tex} ] or {tex}C=\frac{1}{42 \pi} \times 10^{-2} \mathrm{~F}{/tex}
solution_html:
As {tex}P_{a v}=V_{e f f} \cdot I_{e f f} \cos \phi{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}I_{e f f}=\frac{P_{a v}}{V_{e f f} \cos \phi}=\frac{550}{220 \times 0.8}=\frac{25}{8} \mathrm{~A}{/tex}
{tex}R=\frac{P_{a v}}{I_{f f}^{2}}=\frac{550 \times 8 \times 8}{25 \times 25}=\frac{22 \times 64}{25} \Omega{/tex} {tex}\left[\because P_{a v}=I_{e f f}^{2} R\right]{/tex}
{tex}\tan \phi=\frac{\sin \phi}{\cos \phi}=\frac{\sqrt{1-(0.8)^{2}}}{0.8}=\frac{0.6}{0.8}=\frac{3}{4}{/tex}
But {tex}\tan \phi=\frac{X_{L}}{R}{/tex}
{tex}X_{L}=\tan \phi . R=\frac{3}{4} \times \frac{22 \times 64}{25} \simeq 42 \Omega{/tex}
For power factor to be unity.
{tex}X_{L}=X_{C}{/tex} or {tex}\omega L=\frac{1}{\omega C}{/tex}
or {tex}C=\frac{1}{\omega^{2} L}=\frac{1}{\omega X_{L}}=\frac{1}{2 \pi f} \cdot \frac{1}{X_{L}}=\frac{1}{100 \pi} \cdot \frac{1}{42}{/tex}[{tex}\because{/tex} {tex}\omega L=X_{L}{/tex}]
or {tex}C=\frac{1}{42 \pi} \times 10^{-2} \mathrm{~F}{/tex}

question_text: Show graphically the variation of instantaneous power P with angle cot when the alternating voltage V = V 0 sin {tex}\omega{/tex} t is applied across a pure resistor a pure inductor and a pure capacitor.
explanation: Instantaneous voltage and current are in same phase in a pure resistor {tex}V=V_{0} \sin \omega t{/tex} and {tex}I=I_{0} \sin \omega t{/tex} Instantaneous power, P = VI = {tex}V_{0} I_{0} \sin ^{2} \omega t{/tex} Variation of instantaneous power with {tex}\omega t{/tex} in a resistive a.c. circuit. Instantaneous power is +ve at every instant except when both V and I are zero. In a pure inductor, current lags behind voltage by a phase angle of {tex}\pi \over 2{/tex} {tex}V=V_{0} \sin \omega t{/tex} and {tex}I=I_{0} \sin (\omega t-\pi / 2)=-I_{0} \cos \omega t{/tex} Instantaneous power, {tex}P=V I=-V_{0} I_{0} \sin \omega t \cos \omega t=-\frac{V_{0} I_{0}}{2} \sin 2 \omega t{/tex} Variation of instantaneous power with {tex}\omega t{/tex} in an inductive a.c. circuit. In a pure capacitor, voltage lags behind the current by a phase angle of {tex}\frac{\pi}{2}{/tex} . {tex}V=V_{0} \sin \omega t{/tex} , and I = I 0 sin( {tex}\omega{/tex} t + {tex}\pi \over 2{/tex} ) = I 0 cos {tex}\omega{/tex} t Instantaneous power, P = VI = {tex}V_{0} I_{0} \sin \omega t \cos \omega t=\frac{V_{0} I_{0}}{2} \sin 2 \omega t{/tex} Variation of instantaneous power with {tex}\omega t{/tex} in a capacitive a.c. circuit.
solution: Instantaneous voltage and current are in same phase in a pure resistor {tex}V=V_{0} \sin \omega t{/tex} and {tex}I=I_{0} \sin \omega t{/tex} Instantaneous power, P = VI = {tex}V_{0} I_{0} \sin ^{2} \omega t{/tex} Variation of instantaneous power with {tex}\omega t{/tex} in a resistive a.c. circuit. Instantaneous power is +ve at every instant except when both V and I are zero. In a pure inductor, current lags behind voltage by a phase angle of {tex}\pi \over 2{/tex} {tex}V=V_{0} \sin \omega t{/tex} and {tex}I=I_{0} \sin (\omega t-\pi / 2)=-I_{0} \cos \omega t{/tex} Instantaneous power, {tex}P=V I=-V_{0} I_{0} \sin \omega t \cos \omega t=-\frac{V_{0} I_{0}}{2} \sin 2 \omega t{/tex} Variation of instantaneous power with {tex}\omega t{/tex} in an inductive a.c. circuit. In a pure capacitor, voltage lags behind the current by a phase angle of {tex}\frac{\pi}{2}{/tex} . {tex}V=V_{0} \sin \omega t{/tex} , and I = I 0 sin( {tex}\omega{/tex} t + {tex}\pi \over 2{/tex} ) = I 0 cos {tex}\omega{/tex} t Instantaneous power, P = VI = {tex}V_{0} I_{0} \sin \omega t \cos \omega t=\frac{V_{0} I_{0}}{2} \sin 2 \omega t{/tex} Variation of instantaneous power with {tex}\omega t{/tex} in a capacitive a.c. circuit.
solution_html:
1. Instantaneous voltage and current are in same phase in a pure resistor
    {tex}V=V_{0} \sin \omega t{/tex} and {tex}I=I_{0} \sin \omega t{/tex}
    Instantaneous power, P = VI = {tex}V_{0} I_{0} \sin ^{2} \omega t{/tex}

    Variation of instantaneous power with {tex}\omega t{/tex} in a resistive a.c. circuit.
    Instantaneous power is +ve at every instant except when both V and I are zero.
  2. In a pure inductor, current lags behind voltage by a phase angle of {tex}\pi \over 2{/tex}
    {tex}V=V_{0} \sin \omega t{/tex}
    and {tex}I=I_{0} \sin (\omega t-\pi / 2)=-I_{0} \cos \omega t{/tex}
    Instantaneous power,
    {tex}P=V I=-V_{0} I_{0} \sin \omega t \cos \omega t=-\frac{V_{0} I_{0}}{2} \sin 2 \omega t{/tex}
     
    Variation of instantaneous power with {tex}\omega t{/tex} in an inductive a.c. circuit.
  3. In a pure capacitor, voltage lags behind the current by a phase angle of {tex}\frac{\pi}{2}{/tex}.
    {tex}V=V_{0} \sin \omega t{/tex}, and I = I0 sin({tex}\omega{/tex}t + {tex}\pi \over 2{/tex}) = I0 cos{tex}\omega{/tex}t 
    Instantaneous power, 
    P = VI = {tex}V_{0} I_{0} \sin \omega t \cos \omega t=\frac{V_{0} I_{0}}{2} \sin 2 \omega t{/tex}

    Variation of instantaneous power with {tex}\omega t{/tex} in a capacitive a.c. circuit.

question_text: State the principle of working of a transformer. Define the efficiency of a transformer. State any two factors that reduce the efficiency of a transformer. Calculate the current drawn by the primary of a 90% efficient transformer which steps down 220 V to 22 V if the output resistance is 440 {tex}\Omega{/tex} .
explanation: A transformer works on the principle of mutual induction. (Alternatively-an emf is induced in the secondary coil when the magnetic flux, linked with it changes with time due to a (time) changing magnetic flux linked with the primary coil). The efficiency of a transformer equals the ratio of the output power to the input power. The input and output power are measured in the same unit Alternatively: Efficiency = {tex}\frac{\text { output power }}{\text { input power }}{/tex} Efficiency = {tex}\frac{V_{s} I_{s}}{V_{p} I_{p}}{/tex} Efficiency is affected by the factors Eddy current losses joule heat losses hysteresis losses magnetic flux leakage losses Calculating the current drawn We have {tex}\frac{V_{s} I_{s}}{V_{p} I_{p}}{/tex} = 90% = 0.9 {tex}\therefore \frac{22 I_{s}}{220 I_{p}}{/tex} = 0.9 or {tex}\frac{I_{s}}{I_{p}}=\frac{0.9}{0.1}{/tex} = 9 {tex}\therefore{/tex} I p = {tex}\frac{I_{5}}{9}=\frac{\left(\frac{22}{440}\right)}{9}{/tex} A {tex}=\frac{1}{180}{/tex} A = 0.0056 A

    (Alternatively-an emf is induced in the secondary coil when the magnetic flux, linked with it changes with time due to a (time) changing magnetic flux linked with the primary coil).
  2. The efficiency of a transformer equals the ratio of the output power to the input power. The input and output power are measured in the same unit
    Alternatively:
    Efficiency = {tex}\frac{\text { output power }}{\text { input power }}{/tex}
    Efficiency  = {tex}\frac{V_{s} I_{s}}{V_{p} I_{p}}{/tex}
  3. Efficiency is affected by the factors

    {tex}=\frac{1}{180}{/tex} A

question_text: The primary of a transformer has 400 turns while the secondary has 2000 turns. If the power output from the secondary at 1100 V is 12.1 kW, calculate the primary voltage. If the resistance of the primary is 0.2 {tex}\Omega{/tex} and that of the secondary is 2.0 {tex}\Omega{/tex} and the efficiency of the transformer is 90 %, calculate the heat losses in the primary and the secondary coils.
explanation: N 1 = 400, N 2 = 2000, {tex}\varepsilon_{2}{/tex} = 1100 V {tex}\varepsilon_{1}=\varepsilon_{2} \cdot \frac{N_{1}}{N_{2}}=1100 \times \frac{400}{2000}{/tex} = 220V Resistance of primary, = 0.2 {tex}\Omega{/tex} Output power = {tex}\varepsilon_{2} I_{2}{/tex} = 12.1 kW = 12100 W {tex}\therefore{/tex} Current in the secondary, {tex}I_{2}=\frac{\varepsilon_{2} I_{2}}{\varepsilon_{2}}=\frac{12100}{1100}=11 \mathrm{~A}{/tex} As Efficiency = {tex}Output \ power \over Input \ power{/tex} {tex}\frac{90}{100}=\frac{12100 \mathrm{~W}}{\text { Input power }}{/tex} or Input power, {tex}\varepsilon_{1} I_{1}=\frac{12100 \times 100}{90}=13.44 \times 10^{3} \mathrm{~W}{/tex} Current in the primary, {tex}I_1=\frac{\varepsilon_{1} I_{1}}{\varepsilon_{1}}=\frac{13.44 \times 10^{3}}{220}=61.1 \mathrm{~A}{/tex} Power loss in the primary {tex}=I_{1}^{2} R_{1}=(61.1)^{2} \times 0.2{/tex} = 746.61 W Power loss in the secondary {tex}=I_{2}^{2} R_{2}=(11)^{2} \times 2.0{/tex} = 242 W

1. N1 = 400, N2 = 2000, {tex}\varepsilon_{2}{/tex} = 1100 V
    {tex}\varepsilon_{1}=\varepsilon_{2} \cdot \frac{N_{1}}{N_{2}}=1100 \times \frac{400}{2000}{/tex} = 220V
  2. Resistance of primary, = 0.2 {tex}\Omega{/tex}
    Output power = {tex}\varepsilon_{2} I_{2}{/tex} = 12.1 kW = 12100 W
    {tex}\therefore{/tex} Current in the secondary,
    {tex}I_{2}=\frac{\varepsilon_{2} I_{2}}{\varepsilon_{2}}=\frac{12100}{1100}=11 \mathrm{~A}{/tex}
    As Efficiency = {tex}Output \ power \over Input \ power{/tex}
    {tex}\frac{90}{100}=\frac{12100 \mathrm{~W}}{\text { Input power }}{/tex}
    or Input power,
    {tex}\varepsilon_{1} I_{1}=\frac{12100 \times 100}{90}=13.44 \times 10^{3} \mathrm{~W}{/tex}
    Current in the primary,
    {tex}I_1=\frac{\varepsilon_{1} I_{1}}{\varepsilon_{1}}=\frac{13.44 \times 10^{3}}{220}=61.1 \mathrm{~A}{/tex}
    Power loss in the primary
    {tex}=I_{1}^{2} R_{1}=(61.1)^{2} \times 0.2{/tex} = 746.61 W
    Power loss in the secondary
    {tex}=I_{2}^{2} R_{2}=(11)^{2} \times 2.0{/tex} = 242 W

  2. A small town with a demand of 1200 kW of electric power at 220 V is situated 20 km away from an electric plant generating power at 440 V. The resistance of the two wire line carrying power is 0.5{tex}\Omega{/tex} per km. The town gets the power from the line through a 4000-220 V step-down transformer at a sub-station in the town. Estimate the line power loss in the form of heat
question_text: Draw the diagram of a device which is used to decrease high ac voltage into a low ac voltage and state its working principle. Write four sources of energy loss in this device. A small town with a demand of 1200 kW of electric power at 220 V is situated 20 km away from an electric plant generating power at 440 V. The resistance of the two wire line carrying power is 0.5 {tex}\Omega{/tex} per km. The town gets the power from the line through a 4000-220 V step-down transformer at a sub-station in the town. Estimate the line power loss in the form of heat
explanation: The device used to decrease high ac voltage into a low ac voltage is called transformer (step-down transformer). Working principle: Transformer works on the principle of Faraday’s law of electromagnetic induction. The law of electromagnetic induction states that when magnetic flux linked with a coil changes, an emf is induced in the coil. Transformer consists of two coils called primary coil and secondary coil. The ac current in primary coil changes magnetic flux linked with the secondary coil and thus an emf is induced in the secondary coil. Sources of energy loss in transformer Copper loss: The coils of transformer (made of copper) have a finite resistance due to which some energy in lost as heat. Iron loss: Due to induced eddy currents in the iron core, some energy is lost in the bulk. Magnetic loss: Since all magnetic flux in primary coil does not pass through the secondary coil, there is some loss of energy due to leakage of flux. Hysteresis loss: alternating magnetization and demagnetization of the iron core cause some loss of energy in form of heat Demand of electric power = 1200 kW Distance of town from power station = 20 km Two wire = 20 {tex}\times{/tex} 2 = 40 km Total resistance of line = 40 {tex}\times{/tex} 0.5 = 200 The town gets a power of 4000 volts Power = voltage {tex}\times{/tex} current {tex}I=\frac{1200 \times 10^3}{4000}=\frac{1200}{4}=300 \mathrm{~A}{/tex} The line power loss in the form of heat = I 2 {tex}\times{/tex} R = (300) 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 2 = 9000 {tex}\times{/tex} 20 = 1800 kW

solution_html:
1. The device used to decrease high ac voltage into a low ac voltage is called transformer (step-down transformer).
    Working principle:
    Transformer works on the principle of Faraday’s law of electromagnetic induction. The law of electromagnetic induction states that when magnetic flux linked with a coil changes, an emf is induced in the coil. Transformer consists of two coils called primary coil and secondary coil. The ac current in primary coil changes magnetic flux linked with the secondary coil and thus an emf is induced in the secondary coil.

    Sources of energy loss in transformer
    1. Copper loss: The coils of transformer (made of copper) have a finite resistance due to which some energy in lost as heat. 
    2. Iron loss: Due to induced eddy currents in the iron core, some energy is lost in the bulk. 
    3. Magnetic loss: Since all magnetic flux in primary coil does not pass through the secondary coil, there is some loss of energy due to leakage of flux. 
    4. Hysteresis loss: alternating magnetization and demagnetization of the iron core cause some loss of energy in form of heat
  2. Demand of electric power = 1200 kW
    Distance of town from power station = 20 km Two wire = 20 {tex}\times{/tex} 2 = 40 km
    Total resistance of line = 40 {tex}\times{/tex} 0.5 = 200
    The town gets a power of 4000 volts
    Power = voltage {tex}\times{/tex} current
    {tex}I=\frac{1200 \times 10^3}{4000}=\frac{1200}{4}=300 \mathrm{~A}{/tex}
    The line power loss in the form of heat = I2 {tex}\times{/tex} R
    = (300)2 {tex}\times{/tex} 2
    = 9000 {tex}\times{/tex} 20 = 1800 kW

question_html:
1. When an AC source is connected to an ideal capacitor, then show that the average power supplied by the source over a complete cycle is zero.
  2. A lamp is connected in series with a capacitor. Predict your observations when the system is connected first across a DC and then an AC source. What happens in each case if the capacitance of the capacitor is reduced?
question_text: When an AC source is connected to an ideal capacitor, then show that the average power supplied by the source over a complete cycle is zero. A lamp is connected in series with a capacitor. Predict your observations when the system is connected first across a DC and then an AC source. What happens in each case if the capacitance of the capacitor is reduced?
explanation: When a source of AC is connected to a capacitor of capacitance C , the charge on it grows from zero to maximum steady value Q 0 . The energy stored in a capacitor is, {tex}E = \frac { 1 } { 2 } C V _ { 0 } ^ { 2 }{/tex} where, V 0 is maximum potential difference across the plates of the capacitor. The alternating voltage applied is {tex} { V } = { V } _ { 0 } \sin \omega t{/tex} and the current leads the emf by a phase angle of {tex}\pi /2{/tex} {tex}I = I _ { 0 } \sin \left( \omega t + \frac { \pi } { 2 } \right) = I _ { 0 } \cos \omega t{/tex} {tex}\therefore {/tex} Work done over a complete cycle is, {tex}W = \int _ { 0 } ^ { T } V Id t = \int _ { 0 } ^ { T } \left( V_ { 0 } \sin \omega t \right) \left( I _ { 0 } \cos \omega t \right) d t{/tex} {tex}= \frac { V _ { 0 } I _ { 0 } } { 2 } \int _ { 0 } ^ { T } 2 \sin \omega t \cos \omega t d t{/tex} {tex}\therefore W = \frac { V _ { 0 } I _ { 0 } } { 2 } \int _ { 0 } ^ { T } \sin 2 \omega t d t{/tex} {tex}W = \frac { V _ { 0 } I _ { 0 } } { 2 } \left[ 1 - \frac { \cos 2 \omega t } { 2 \omega } \right] _ { 0 } ^ { T } = 0{/tex} Now as the work done is zero, hence the average power is also zero (as, time can never be zero). When a DC source is connected, the condenser is charged but no current flows in the circuit. Because a condenser offers infinite resistance for a DC source and never allows a DC current to flow through it. Therefore, the lamp does not glow. No change occurs even when capacitance of capacitor is reduced. Now when an AC source is connected with the circuit, the capacitor offers capacitive reactance {tex}X _ { C } = 1 / \omega C{/tex} (where {tex}\omega {/tex} is the angular frequency of the applied AC voltage). The current flows in the circuit and the lamp glows. On reducing the value of C , the capacitive reactance, X C increases. Therefore, the glowing of the bulb reduces.

solution_html:
1. When a source of AC is connected to a capacitor of capacitance C, the charge on it grows from zero to maximum steady value Q0 . 
    The energy stored in a capacitor is, {tex}E = \frac { 1 } { 2 } C V _ { 0 } ^ { 2 }{/tex} where, V0 is maximum potential difference across the plates of the capacitor.
    The alternating voltage applied is
    {tex} { V } = { V } _ { 0 } \sin \omega t{/tex}
    and the current leads the emf by a phase angle of {tex}\pi /2{/tex}
    {tex}I = I _ { 0 } \sin \left( \omega t + \frac { \pi } { 2 } \right) = I _ { 0 } \cos \omega t{/tex}
    {tex}\therefore {/tex} Work done over a complete cycle is,
    {tex}W = \int _ { 0 } ^ { T } V Id t = \int _ { 0 } ^ { T } \left( V_ { 0 } \sin \omega t \right) \left( I _ { 0 } \cos \omega t \right) d t{/tex}
    {tex}= \frac { V _ { 0 } I _ { 0 } } { 2 } \int _ { 0 } ^ { T } 2 \sin \omega t \cos \omega t d t{/tex}
    {tex}\therefore W = \frac { V _ { 0 } I _ { 0 } } { 2 } \int _ { 0 } ^ { T } \sin 2 \omega t d t{/tex}
    {tex}W = \frac { V _ { 0 } I _ { 0 } } { 2 } \left[ 1 - \frac { \cos 2 \omega t } { 2 \omega } \right] _ { 0 } ^ { T } = 0{/tex}
    Now as the work done is zero, hence the average power is also zero (as, time can never be zero).
  2. When a DC source is connected, the condenser is charged but no current flows in the circuit. Because a condenser offers infinite resistance for a DC source and never allows a DC current to flow through it. Therefore, the lamp does not glow. No change occurs even when capacitance of capacitor is reduced.
    Now when an AC source is connected with the circuit, the capacitor offers capacitive reactance {tex}X _ { C } = 1 / \omega C{/tex} (where {tex}\omega {/tex} is the angular frequency of the applied AC voltage). The current flows in the circuit and the lamp glows. On reducing the value of C, the capacitive reactance, XC increases. Therefore, the glowing of the bulb reduces.

The resistance of the two wire line carrying power is 0.5 {tex}\Omega{/tex} per km. By replacement of the earlier transformer by a 40,000 - 220 V step-down transformer (Neglect, as before, leakage losses though this may not be a good assumption any longer because of the very high voltage transmission involved). Hence, explain why high voltage transmission is preferred?
question_text: A small town with a demand of 800 kW of electric power at 220 V is situated 15 km away from an electric plant generating power at 440 V. The resistance of the two wire line carrying power is 0.5 {tex}\Omega{/tex} per km. By replacement of the earlier transformer by a 40,000 - 220 V step-down transformer (Neglect, as before, leakage losses though this may not be a good assumption any longer because of the very high voltage transmission involved). Hence, explain why high voltage transmission is preferred?
explanation: Power loss is minimum when voltage is high this can be explained as:- The rating of a step-down transformer is 40000 V - 220 V. Input voitage is given by, V 1 = 40000 V Output voltage is given by, V 2 = 220 V and Total electric power required, P = 800 KW = 800 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 3 W Source potential, V = 220 V Voltage at which the electric plant generates power, V' = 440 V Distance between the town and power generating station is given by, d = 15 Km Resistance of the two wire lines carrying power = 0.5 {tex}\Omega{/tex} /Km Total resistance of the wire lines, R = (15 + 15) 0.5 = 15 {tex}\Omega{/tex} P = V 1 I Rms current in the wire line is given as: {tex}I=\frac{P}{V_{1}}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{800 \times 10^{3}}{40000}=20 \mathrm{A}{/tex} Line power loss due to heat is given by = I 2 R = (20) 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 15 = 6 KW Assuming that the power loss is negligible due to the leakage of current. Hence, power supplied by the plant = 800 KW + 6 KW = 806 KW Voltage drop in the power line = IR = 20 {tex}\times{/tex} 15 = 300 V Hence, voltage that is transmitted by the power plant = 300 + 40000 = 40300 V The power is being generated in the plant at 440 V. Hence, the rating of the step-up transformer needed at the plant is 440 V = 40300 V. Hence, power loss during transmission is {tex}=\frac{600}{1400} \times 100{/tex} = 42.8% In the previous exercise, the power loss due to the same reason is = {tex}\frac{6}{806} \times 100{/tex} = 0.744%. Thus we get that the power loss is less for a high voltage transmission, high voltage transmissions are preferred for this purpose.

solution_html:
Power loss is minimum when voltage is high this can be explained as:-
The rating of a step-down transformer is 40000 V - 220 V.
Input voitage is given by, V1 = 40000 V
Output voltage is given by, V2 = 220 V and Total electric power required, P = 800 KW = 800 {tex}\times{/tex} 103 W
Source potential, V = 220 V
Voltage at which the electric plant generates power, V' = 440 V
Distance between the town and power generating station is given by, d = 15 Km
Resistance of the two wire lines carrying power = 0.5 {tex}\Omega{/tex}/Km
Total resistance of the wire lines, R = (15 + 15) 0.5 = 15 {tex}\Omega{/tex}
P = V1I
Rms current in the wire line is given as:
{tex}I=\frac{P}{V_{1}}{/tex}
{tex}=\frac{800 \times 10^{3}}{40000}=20 \mathrm{A}{/tex}

  1. Line power loss due to heat is given by = I2R
    = (20)2 {tex}\times{/tex} 15
    = 6 KW
  2. Assuming that the power loss is negligible due to the leakage of current. 
    Hence, power supplied by the plant = 800 KW + 6 KW = 806 KW
  3. Voltage drop in the power line = IR = 20 {tex}\times{/tex} 15 = 300 V
    Hence, voltage that is transmitted by the power plant 
    = 300 + 40000 = 40300 V
    The power is being generated in the plant at 440 V.
    Hence, the rating of the step-up transformer needed at the plant is
    440 V = 40300 V.
    Hence, power loss during transmission is {tex}=\frac{600}{1400} \times 100{/tex} = 42.8%
    In the previous exercise, the power loss due to the same reason is = {tex}\frac{6}{806} \times 100{/tex} = 0.744%. Thus we get that the power loss is less for a high voltage transmission, high voltage transmissions are preferred for this purpose.

1. Draw a labelled diagram of a step-up transformer and describe its working principle. Explain any three causes for energy losses in a real transformer.
  2. A step-up transformer converts a low voltage into high voltage. Does it violate the principle of conservation of energy? Explain.
  3. A step-up transformer has 200 and 3000 turns in its primary and secondary coils respectively. The input voltage given to the primary coil is 90 V. Calculate:
    1. The output voltage across the secondary coil
    2. The current in the primary coil if the current in the
      secondary coil is 2.0 A.
question_text: Draw a labelled diagram of a step-up transformer and describe its working principle. Explain any three causes for energy losses in a real transformer. A step-up transformer converts a low voltage into high voltage. Does it violate the principle of conservation of energy? Explain. A step-up transformer has 200 and 3000 turns in its primary and secondary coils respectively. The input voltage given to the primary coil is 90 V. Calculate: The output voltage across the secondary coil The current in the primary coil if the current in the secondary coil is 2.0 A.
explanation: The working principle of transformer is mutual induction. When an alternating voltage is applied to the primary, the resulting current produces an alternating magnetic flux which links the secondary and induces an emf in it. Causes of energy losses Copper Losses (I 2 R Losses): Explanation: These losses occur due to the resistance of the wire in the coils. When current flows through the wire, some energy is dissipated as heat due to the resistance. Mitigation: Using thicker wires with lower resistance and cooling mechanisms can help reduce these losses. Core Losses (Iron Losses): Explanation: Core losses include hysteresis loss and eddy current loss. Hysteresis loss occurs due to the lagging of magnetic domains behind the changing magnetic field. Eddy current loss is caused by circulating currents induced within the core material due to the changing magnetic field. Mitigation: Using laminated silicon steel cores reduces eddy currents, and using materials with low hysteresis loss minimizes core losses. Leakage Flux: Explanation: Not all magnetic flux produced by the primary coil links with the secondary coil. Some flux "leaks" and does not contribute to energy transfer between coils, leading to inefficiency. Mitigation: Improving core design and using better core materials can help reduce leakage flux. A step-up transformer does not violate the conservation of energy. It increases voltage by decreasing current proportionally. The power input (low voltage, high current) equals the power output (high voltage, low current), ensuring energy conservation. The transformer merely transfers energy, with losses typically due to inefficiencies. {tex}\frac{V_s}{V_P}=\frac{N_s}{N_P}{/tex} {tex}V_s=\frac{N_s}{N_P} \times V_P=\frac{3000}{200} \times 90{/tex} {tex}V_s=1350 \mathrm{~V}{/tex} {tex}\frac{I_P}{I_s}=\frac{N_s}{N_P}{/tex} {tex}I_P=\frac{3000}{200} \times 2=30 \mathrm{~A}{/tex}

When an alternating voltage is applied to the primary, the resulting current produces an alternating magnetic flux which links the secondary and induces an emf in it. Causes of energy losses Copper Losses (I 2 R Losses): Explanation: These losses occur due to the resistance of the wire in the coils. When current flows through the wire, some energy is dissipated as heat due to the resistance. Mitigation: Using thicker wires with lower resistance and cooling mechanisms can help reduce these losses. Core Losses (Iron Losses): Explanation: Core losses include hysteresis loss and eddy current loss. Hysteresis loss occurs due to the lagging of magnetic domains behind the changing magnetic field. Eddy current loss is caused by circulating currents induced within the core material due to the changing magnetic field. Mitigation: Using laminated silicon steel cores reduces eddy currents, and using materials with low hysteresis loss minimizes core losses. Leakage Flux: Explanation: Not all magnetic flux produced by the primary coil links with the secondary coil. Some flux "leaks" and does not contribute to energy transfer between coils, leading to inefficiency. Mitigation: Improving core design and using better core materials can help reduce leakage flux. solution_html:
1. The working principle of transformer is mutual induction.
    When an alternating voltage is applied to the primary, the resulting current produces an alternating magnetic flux which links the secondary and induces an emf in it.
    Causes of energy losses
    1. Copper Losses (I2R Losses):
      Explanation: These losses occur due to the resistance of the wire in the coils. When current flows through the wire, some energy is dissipated as heat due to the resistance.
      Mitigation: Using thicker wires with lower resistance and cooling mechanisms can help reduce these losses.
    2. Core Losses (Iron Losses):
      Explanation: Core losses include hysteresis loss and eddy current loss. Hysteresis loss occurs due to the lagging of magnetic domains behind the changing magnetic field. Eddy current loss is caused by circulating currents induced within the core material due to the changing magnetic field.
      Mitigation: Using laminated silicon steel cores reduces eddy currents, and using materials with low hysteresis loss minimizes core losses.
    3. Leakage Flux:
      Explanation: Not all magnetic flux produced by the primary coil links with the secondary coil. Some flux "leaks" and does not contribute to energy transfer between coils, leading to inefficiency.
      Mitigation: Improving core design and using better core materials can help reduce leakage flux.
  2. A step-up transformer does not violate the conservation of energy. It increases voltage by decreasing current proportionally. The power input (low voltage, high current) equals the power output (high voltage, low current), ensuring energy conservation. The transformer merely transfers energy, with losses typically due to inefficiencies.
    1. {tex}\frac{V_s}{V_P}=\frac{N_s}{N_P}{/tex}
      {tex}V_s=\frac{N_s}{N_P} \times V_P=\frac{3000}{200} \times 90{/tex}
      {tex}V_s=1350 \mathrm{~V}{/tex}
    2. {tex}\frac{I_P}{I_s}=\frac{N_s}{N_P}{/tex}
      {tex}I_P=\frac{3000}{200} \times 2=30 \mathrm{~A}{/tex}

  2. The coil of an ac generator consists of 100 turns of wire, each of area 0.5 m2. The resistance of the wire is 100 {tex}\Omega{/tex}. Calculate the maximum emf generated and power dissipated in the coil.
question_text: With the help of a labelled diagram, describe the principle and working of an ac generator. Hence, obtain an expression for the instantaneous value of the emf generated. The coil of an ac generator consists of 100 turns of wire, each of area 0.5 m 2 . The resistance of the wire is 100 {tex}\Omega{/tex} . The coil is rotating in a magnetic field of 0.8 T perpendicular to its axis of rotation, at a constant angular speed of 60 radian per second. Calculate the maximum emf generated and power dissipated in the coil.
explanation: Diagram Principle - It is based on the principle of electromagnetic induction. Whenever there is a change in magnetic flux linked with a coil , an emf is induced in the coil. Working - When a rectangular coil is rotated in a magnetic field, the magnetic flux changes continuously which induces an emf and the direction of current changes periodically. {tex}\varepsilon=\frac{-N d \phi}{d t}{/tex} {tex}=-N B A \frac{d}{d t}(\cos \omega t){/tex} {tex}\varepsilon=N B A \omega \sin \omega t{/tex} {tex}\varepsilon=N B A \omega{/tex} = 100 {tex}\times{/tex} 0.8 {tex}\times{/tex} 0.5 {tex}\times{/tex} 60 = 2400 V hence, the maximum emf generated will be 2400 V. Power dissipated, P = {tex}\frac{\varepsilon_{r m s}^2}{R}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{\left(\frac{2400}{\sqrt{2}}\right)^2}{100}{/tex} = 28.8 kW

Power dissipated, P = {tex}\frac{\varepsilon_{r m s}^2}{R}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{\left(\frac{2400}{\sqrt{2}}\right)^2}{100}{/tex} = 28.8 kW

1. Diagram

    Principle - It is based on the principle of electromagnetic induction. Whenever there is a change in magnetic flux linked with a coil , an emf is induced in the coil.
    Working - When a rectangular coil is rotated in a magnetic field, the magnetic flux changes continuously which induces an emf and the direction of current changes periodically.
    {tex}\varepsilon=\frac{-N d \phi}{d t}{/tex}
    {tex}=-N B A \frac{d}{d t}(\cos \omega t){/tex}
    {tex}\varepsilon=N B A \omega \sin \omega t{/tex}
  2. {tex}\varepsilon=N B A \omega{/tex}
    = 100 {tex}\times{/tex} 0.8 {tex}\times{/tex} 0.5 {tex}\times{/tex} 60
    = 2400 V
    hence, the maximum emf generated will be 2400 V. 
    Power dissipated, P = {tex}\frac{\varepsilon_{r m s}^2}{R}{/tex}
    {tex}=\frac{\left(\frac{2400}{\sqrt{2}}\right)^2}{100}{/tex}
    = 28.8 kW

  2. Given the input current 15 A and the input voltage of 100 V for a step-up transformer having 90% efficiency, find the output power and the voltage in the secondary if the output current is 3 A.
question_text: Describe, with the help of a suitable diagram, the working principle of a step-up transformer. Obtain the relation between input and output voltages in terms of the number of turns of primary and secondary windings and the currents in the input and output circuits. Given the input current 15 A and the input voltage of 100 V for a step-up transformer having 90% efficiency, find the output power and the voltage in the secondary if the output current is 3 A.
explanation: Working principle: Step-down transformer is made up of two or more coil wound on the iron core of the transformer. It works on the principle of magnetic induction between the coils. Whenever current in one coil changes an emf gets induced in the neighboring coil (Principle of mutual induction) Voltage across secondary V s = e s = -N s {tex}\frac{d \phi}{d t}{/tex} Voltage across primary V p = e p = -N p {tex}\frac{d \phi}{d t}{/tex} {tex}\frac{V_{s}}{V_{p}}=\frac{N_{s}}{N_{p}}{/tex} (here, N s > N p ) In an ideal transformer Power Input - Power output I p V p = I s V s {tex}\therefore \frac{V_{s}}{V_{p}}=\frac{N_{s}}{N_{p}}=\frac{I_{p}}{I_{s}}{/tex} Input power, P i = I i {tex}\times{/tex} V i = 15 {tex}\times{/tex} 100 = 1500 W Power output, P 0 = P i {tex}\times \frac{90}{100}{/tex} = 1350 W {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} I 0 V = 1350 W Output voltage, V 0 = {tex}\frac{1350}{3}{/tex} V = 450 V

    Step-down transformer is made up of two or more coil wound on the iron core of the transformer. It works on the principle of magnetic induction between the coils. Whenever current in one coil changes an emf gets induced in the neighboring coil (Principle of mutual induction)
    Voltage across secondary
    Vs = es = -Ns {tex}\frac{d \phi}{d t}{/tex}
    Voltage across primary
    Vp = ep = -Np {tex}\frac{d \phi}{d t}{/tex}
    {tex}\frac{V_{s}}{V_{p}}=\frac{N_{s}}{N_{p}}{/tex} (here, Ns > Np)
    In an ideal transformer

    {tex}\therefore \frac{V_{s}}{V_{p}}=\frac{N_{s}}{N_{p}}=\frac{I_{p}}{I_{s}}{/tex}

    cos {tex}\phi{/tex} = {tex}\frac{R}{Z}{/tex}

    {tex}\therefore{/tex} cos {tex}\phi{/tex} = 0

      {tex}\therefore{/tex} L = {tex}\frac{X_L}{2 \pi f}{/tex}

1. Write the function of a transformer. State its principle of working with the help of a diagram. Mention various energy losses in this device.
  2. The primary coil of an ideal step-up transformer has 100 turns and transformation ratio is also 100. The input voltage and power are respectively 220 V and 1100 W. Calculate
    1. number of turns in secondary
    2. current in primary
    3. voltage across secondary
    4. current in secondary
    5. power in secondary
question_text: Write the function of a transformer. State its principle of working with the help of a diagram. Mention various energy losses in this device. The primary coil of an ideal step-up transformer has 100 turns and transformation ratio is also 100. The input voltage and power are respectively 220 V and 1100 W. Calculate number of turns in secondary current in primary voltage across secondary current in secondary power in secondary
explanation: A transformer is a device that changes a low alternating voltage into a high alternating voltage or vice versa. The transformer works on the principle of mutual induction. A changing alternate current in the primary coil produces a changing magnetic field, which induces a changing alternating current in the secondary coil. Energy losses in the transformer: a. Flux leakage due to poor structure of the core and air gaps in the core. b. Loss of energy due to heat produced by the resistance of the windings. c. Eddy currents due to alternating magnetic flux in the iron core, which leads to loss of energy due to heat. d. Hysterisis, frequent and periodic magnetisation and demagnetisation of the core, leading to loss of energy due to heat. Now, N = {tex}\frac{N_{s}}{N_{p}}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{N_{S}}{100}{/tex} = 100 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} N S = 10000 turns Current in primary is given by, I P V P = P {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} I P = {tex}\frac{1100}{220}{/tex} = 5A Voltage across secondary is given by, {tex}\frac{V_{s}}{V_{p}}=\frac{N_{s}}{N_{p}}{/tex} = N {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} V S = 100 {tex}\times{/tex} 220 = 22000 V Current in secondary is given by V S I S = P {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} I S = {tex}\frac{P}{V_{s}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1100}{22000}{/tex} = 0.05 A In an ideal transformer, Power in secondary = Power in primary = 1100 W

A changing alternate current in the primary coil produces a changing magnetic field, which induces a changing alternating current in the secondary coil. Energy losses in the transformer: a. Flux leakage due to poor structure of the core and air gaps in the core. b. Loss of energy due to heat produced by the resistance of the windings. c. Eddy currents due to alternating magnetic flux in the iron core, which leads to loss of energy due to heat. d. Hysterisis, frequent and periodic magnetisation and demagnetisation of the core, leading to loss of energy due to heat. Now, N = {tex}\frac{N_{s}}{N_{p}}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{N_{S}}{100}{/tex} = 100 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} N S = 10000 turns Current in primary is given by, I P V P = P {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} I P = {tex}\frac{1100}{220}{/tex} = 5A Voltage across secondary is given by, {tex}\frac{V_{s}}{V_{p}}=\frac{N_{s}}{N_{p}}{/tex} = N {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} V S = 100 {tex}\times{/tex} 220 = 22000 V Current in secondary is given by V S I S = P {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} I S = {tex}\frac{P}{V_{s}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1100}{22000}{/tex} = 0.05 A In an ideal transformer, Power in secondary = Power in primary = 1100 W
solution_html:
1. A transformer is a device that changes a low alternating voltage into a high alternating voltage or vice versa. The transformer works on the principle of mutual induction. A changing alternate current in the primary coil produces a changing magnetic field, which induces a changing alternating current in the secondary coil.

    Energy losses in the transformer:
    a. Flux leakage due to poor structure of the core and air gaps in the core. 
    b. Loss of energy due to heat produced by the resistance of the windings.
    c. Eddy currents due to alternating magnetic flux in the iron core, which leads to loss of energy due to heat.
    d. Hysterisis, frequent and periodic magnetisation and demagnetisation of the core, leading to loss of energy due to heat.
    1. Now,
      N = {tex}\frac{N_{s}}{N_{p}}{/tex}
      {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{N_{S}}{100}{/tex} = 100
      {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} NS = 10000 turns
    2. Current in primary is given by,
      IPVP = P
      {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} IP = {tex}\frac{1100}{220}{/tex} = 5A
    3. Voltage across secondary is given by,
      {tex}\frac{V_{s}}{V_{p}}=\frac{N_{s}}{N_{p}}{/tex} = N
      {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} VS = 100 {tex}\times{/tex} 220 = 22000 V
    4. Current in secondary is given by
      VSIS = P
      {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} IS = {tex}\frac{P}{V_{s}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1100}{22000}{/tex} = 0.05 A
    5. In an ideal transformer, 
      Power in secondary = Power in primary = 1100 W

An emf {tex}\varepsilon{/tex} = 100 sin 314 t is applied across a pure capacitor of 637 {tex}\mu{/tex}F. Find

  1. the instantaneous current I
  2. instantaneous power P
  3. the frequency of power and
  4. the maximum energy stored in the capacitor.
question_text: An emf {tex}\varepsilon{/tex} = 100 sin 314 t is applied across a pure capacitor of 637 {tex}\mu{/tex} F. Find the instantaneous current I instantaneous power P the frequency of power and the maximum energy stored in the capacitor.
explanation: Given {tex}\varepsilon{/tex} = 100 sin 314 t volt As the current in a capacitor leads the voltage by 90 o , so the instantaneous current is given by I = {tex}I_{0} \sin \left(314 t+90^{\circ}\right)=I_{0} \cos 314 t{/tex} where {tex}I_{0}=\frac{\varepsilon_{0}}{X_{C}}=\frac{\varepsilon_{0}}{1 / \omega C}=\varepsilon_{0} \omega C{/tex} But {tex}\varepsilon_{0}{/tex} = 100 V, {tex}\omega=314{/tex} rads -1 , C = {tex}637 \times 10^{-6} \mathrm{~F}{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}I_{0}=100 \times 314 \times 637 \times 10^{-6}=20 \mathrm{~A}{/tex} Where {tex}I_{0}=\frac{\varepsilon_{0}}{X_{C}}=\frac{\varepsilon_{0}}{1 / \omega C}=\varepsilon_{0} \omega C{/tex} But {tex}\varepsilon_{0}{/tex} = 100 V, {tex}\omega=314 \text { rad } s^{-1}{/tex} , {tex}C=637 \times 10^{-6} \mathrm{~F}{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}I_{0}=100 \times 314 \times 637 \times 10^{-6}=20 \mathrm{~A}{/tex} Hence I = 20 cos 314 t ampere Instantaneous power, P = {tex}\varepsilon I=100 \sin 314 t \times 20 \cos 314 t{/tex} = 1000 sin 628 t watt Angular frequency of power, {tex}\omega_{p}=628{/tex} rads -1 {tex}\therefore{/tex} Frequency of power, {tex}f_{p}=\frac{\omega_{p}}{2 \pi}=\frac{628}{2 \pi}=100 \mathrm{~Hz}{/tex} The maximum energy stored in the capacitor is U 0 = {tex}\frac{1}{2} C E_{0}^{2}=\frac{1}{2} \times 637 \times 10^{-6} \times(100)^{2}{/tex} = 3.185 J

solution_html:
1. Given {tex}\varepsilon{/tex} = 100 sin 314 t volt
    As the current in a capacitor leads the voltage by 90o, so the instantaneous current is given by
    I = {tex}I_{0} \sin \left(314 t+90^{\circ}\right)=I_{0} \cos 314 t{/tex}
    where {tex}I_{0}=\frac{\varepsilon_{0}}{X_{C}}=\frac{\varepsilon_{0}}{1 / \omega C}=\varepsilon_{0} \omega C{/tex}
    But {tex}\varepsilon_{0}{/tex} = 100 V, {tex}\omega=314{/tex} rads-1, C = {tex}637 \times 10^{-6} \mathrm{~F}{/tex}
    {tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}I_{0}=100 \times 314 \times 637 \times 10^{-6}=20 \mathrm{~A}{/tex}
    Where {tex}I_{0}=\frac{\varepsilon_{0}}{X_{C}}=\frac{\varepsilon_{0}}{1 / \omega C}=\varepsilon_{0} \omega C{/tex}
    But {tex}\varepsilon_{0}{/tex} = 100 V, {tex}\omega=314 \text { rad } s^{-1}{/tex}, {tex}C=637 \times 10^{-6} \mathrm{~F}{/tex}
    {tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}I_{0}=100 \times 314 \times 637 \times 10^{-6}=20 \mathrm{~A}{/tex}
    Hence I = 20 cos 314 t ampere
  2. Instantaneous power,
    P = {tex}\varepsilon I=100 \sin 314 t \times 20 \cos 314 t{/tex}
    = 1000 sin 628 t watt
  3. Angular frequency of power, {tex}\omega_{p}=628{/tex} rads-1
    {tex}\therefore{/tex} Frequency of power, {tex}f_{p}=\frac{\omega_{p}}{2 \pi}=\frac{628}{2 \pi}=100 \mathrm{~Hz}{/tex}
  4. The maximum energy stored in the capacitor is
    U0 = {tex}\frac{1}{2} C E_{0}^{2}=\frac{1}{2} \times 637 \times 10^{-6} \times(100)^{2}{/tex} = 3.185 J

subject: Physics
question_html:
A small town with a demand of 800 kW of electric power at 220 V is situated 15 km away from an electric plant generating power at 440 V. The resistance of the two wirelines carrying power is 0.5 {tex}\Omega{/tex} per km. The town gets power from the line through a 4000 - 220 V step-down transformer at a sub-station in the town.

  1. Estimate the line power loss in the form of heat.
  2. How much power must the plant supply, assuming there is negligible power loss due to leakage?
  3. Characterize the step-up transformer at the plant.
question_text: A small town with a demand of 800 kW of electric power at 220 V is situated 15 km away from an electric plant generating power at 440 V. The resistance of the two wirelines carrying power is 0.5 {tex}\Omega{/tex} per km. The town gets power from the line through a 4000 - 220 V step-down transformer at a sub-station in the town. Estimate the line power loss in the form of heat. How much power must the plant supply, assuming there is negligible power loss due to leakage? Characterize the step-up transformer at the plant.
explanation: Total electric power required, Supply voltage, V = 220 V The voltage at which electric plant is generating power, V' = 440 V Distance between the town and power generating station, d = 15 km The resistance of the two-wire lines carrying power = 0.5 {tex}\Omega{/tex} /km Total resistance of the wires, R = (15 + 15) 0.5 = 15 {tex}\Omega{/tex} A step-down transformer of rating 4000 - 220 V is used in the sub-station. Input voltage is , V 1 = 4000 V Output voltage is, V 2 = 220 V Rms current in the wire lines is given as: {tex}I=\frac{P}{V_{1}}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{800 \times 10^{3}}{4000}{/tex} = 200 A Line power loss in the form of heat is given by = I 2 R = (200) 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 15 = 600 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 3 W = 600 KW Assuming that the power loss is negligible due to the leakage of the current: hence Total power supplied by the plant = 800 KW + 600 KW = 1400 KW Voltage drop in the power line is given by = IR = 200 {tex}\times{/tex} 15 = 3000 V Hence, total voltage transmitted by the plant = 3000 + 4000 = 7000 V Hence, the rating of the step-up transformer situated at the power plant is 440 V - 7000 V.
